message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-REL-1 (high, dim CONC): SIGTERM handler swallows process termination when no prior Python handler exists
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:148-156 (_install_flush_on_exit._handler)
Claim: When tracing is bootstrapped on the main thread and no prior Python SIGTERM handler is installed, signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) returns signal.SIG_DFL, which is NOT callable. The installed _handler flushes/shuts down the span provider and then evaluates `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)` — which is False for SIG_DFL — so it returns WITHOUT terminating. Because it replaced the default SIGTERM disposition (terminate the process), the process no longer dies on SIGTERM. Verified: `python -c "import signal; p=signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM); print(callable(p), p is signal.SIG_DFL)"` prints `False True`. bootstrap_tracing() runs inside run_study() and orchestration.run()/resume(), so any normally-started traced run on the main thread installs this handler.
Why real: SIGTERM is the standard graceful-stop signal: `docker stop`, `kubectl delete pod`, systemd `stop`, and plain `kill` all send it. After this handler is installed, the process ignores SIGTERM's terminate action and keeps running until the orchestrator escalates to SIGKILL after its grace period (typically 10-30s). Every run hangs on shutdown, and an operator's `kill <pid>` silently fails. The unit test (tests/test_observability.py:211-229) even monkeypatches getsignal to SIG_DFL and invokes the handler, but only asserts flush/shutdown were called — it never asserts the process would still terminate, so the defect is uncaught. This directly contradicts the docstring intent ("a main-thread SIGTERM handler also flushes+shuts down"): it flushes but then prevents termination.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request

content.text:
The code matches the finding exactly. The `_handler` flushes and shuts down, then only re-raises/delegates to `prior` if it's callable. When `prior` is `SIG_DFL` (not callable), the handler returns without terminating the process. This is a real behavioral defect: the default SIGTERM disposition (terminate) is replaced with a handler that, in the common case of no prior handler, swallows the signal.

Let me verify the claim about SIG_DFL not being callable, and confirm bootstrap_tracing runs on the main thread in real run paths.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python -c "import signal; p=signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM); print('callable:', callable(p), 'is SIG_DFL:', p is signal.SIG_DFL)"

67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None
110	
111

content.text:
The finding is confirmed. The behavioral chain holds:

1. `SIG_DFL` is not callable (verified: `callable: False is SIG_DFL: True`).
2. `bootstrap_tracing()` is called from `pipeline.py:381`, `graph.py:111`, and `graph.py:139` — real run/resume paths.
3. `_install_flush_on_exit` only installs the handler when on the main thread (the normal case for a CLI/orchestrator entrypoint).
4. The installed `_handler` flushes and shuts down, then `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)` — when `prior` is `SIG_DFL`, this branch is skipped, so the handler returns without re-raising or terminating. The default "terminate the process" disposition is gone.

The result: after tracing bootstraps, a SIGTERM no longer terminates the process. It just flushes spans and continues running. `docker stop` / `kubectl delete pod` / `kill <pid>` will hang until SIGKILL escalation.

Let me confirm the test does not catch termination, and check whether tracing is enabled by default (since `_disabled()` gates installation).

content.text:
The test (lines 226-229) confirms the finding's claim: it monkeypatches `getsignal` to `SIG_DFL`, installs the handler, invokes it, and asserts only `shutdown >= 1`. It never asserts the process would still terminate. The defect is uncaught.

Let me check `_disabled()` to confirm tracing is on by default (otherwise the handler wouldn't install in the common case).

content.text:
Tracing is enabled by default (`_disabled()` only true with an explicit opt-out env var). So in normal operation the handler installs.

The finding holds on every point I can verify:

1. SIG_DFL is not callable — confirmed empirically (`callable: False is SIG_DFL: True`).
2. The handler at tracing.py:150-154 flushes/shuts down then only delegates `if callable(prior)` — when no prior handler exists (`prior is SIG_DFL`), it returns without terminating, replacing SIGTERM's default terminate disposition.
3. Tracing is on by default; `bootstrap_tracing()` runs in real run/resume paths (pipeline.py:381, graph.py:111/139); the handler installs only on the main thread, which is the normal entrypoint case.
4. The test (test_observability.py:211-229) sets up exactly this SIG_DFL scenario but only asserts flush/shutdown were called, never that termination would still occur — so the defect is uncaught.

I could find no mitigation: no `os._exit`, no re-raise, no `signal.raise_signal`, no restoration of default disposition in the no-prior-handler path. This is a genuine production reliability defect (graceful shutdown / SIGTERM is broken whenever tracing is active and no prior SIGTERM handler exists), not a false positive, not out-of-scope-by-design, and not already mitigated.

input.reasoning:
I attempted to refute finding K-REL-1 but it holds on every verifiable point.

CODE (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158): _install_flush_on_exit captures `prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` and installs `_handler`, which does `_safe(flush)`, `_safe(provider.shutdown)`, then `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. There is NO else branch — no os._exit, no signal.raise_signal, no re-raise, no restoration of default disposition. When no prior Python handler exists, prior is signal.SIG_DFL.

VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY: `python3 -c "import signal; p=signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM); ..."` prints `callable: False is SIG_DFL: True`. So in the common no-prior-handler case, the `if callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the handler returns. The default SIGTERM disposition (terminate the process) has been replaced by a handler that flushes and then keeps running. SIGTERM is effectively swallowed.

REACHABILITY: bootstrap_tracing() is invoked in real run/resume paths — pipeline.py:381, orchestration/graph.py:111 and :139. Tracing is ENABLED BY DEFAULT: _disabled() (tracing.py:53-54) returns True only when ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING is explicitly set. The handler is installed whenever bootstrap runs on the main thread (threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()), which is the normal CLI/orchestrator entrypoint case.

TEST GAP CONFIRMED: tests/test_observability.py:211-229 monkeypatches getsignal to SIG_DFL, installs the handler, invokes it, and asserts only calls["shutdown"] >= 1 (and flush). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the regression is uncaught — exactly as the finding states.

NO MITIGATION FOUND: grepped/read the handler region; nothing restores termination in the SIG_DFL path. This is not a false positive, not already-mitigated, not out-of-scope-by-design (it is core process-lifecycle behavior, not dataset/backend-specific), and is a genuine production reliability defect: docker stop / kubectl delete pod / systemd stop / plain kill send SIGTERM, which will now be swallowed until SIGKILL escalation. Real, high-severity, CONC dimension. The only nuance softening "every run hangs" is that an operator could set ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING or another library could have pre-installed a SIGTERM handler; neither is the default, so the defect manifests in normal operation. Finding STANDS.

content.text: Finding K-REL-1 STANDS. The SIGTERM handler at tracing.py:150-154 swallows process termination when no prior Python SIGTERM handler exists (the common case), because SIG_DFL is not callable and the handler has no fallback termination path. Verified empirically, reachable via default-enabled tracing in real run/resume paths, and the test at test_observability.py:211-229 fails to catch it.
